2605.17830.title: Remembering More, Risking More: Longitudinal Safety Risks in Memory-Equipped LLM Agents
2605.17830.paper_summary: Safety evaluations of memory-equipped LLM agents typically measure within-task safety: whether an agent completes a single scenario safely, often under adversarial conditions such as prompt injection or memory poisoning. In deployment, however, a single agent serves many independent tasks over a long horizon, and memory accumulated during earlier tasks can affect behavior on later, unrelated ones. Studying this regime requires evaluation along the temporal dimension across tasks: not whether an agent is safe at any single memory state, but how its safety profile changes as memory accumulates across many independent interactions. We call this failure mode temporal memory contamination. To isolate memory exposure from stream non-stationarity, we introduce a trigger-probe protocol that evaluates a fixed probe set against read-only memory snapshots at varying prefix lengths, together with a NullMemory counterfactual baseline for identifying memory-induced violations. We apply this protocol across three deployment scenarios spanning records, memos, forms, and email correspondence and eight memory architectures, and additionally on Claw-like AI agents, such as OpenClaw, using the platform's native memory mechanism. Memory-enabled agents consistently exceed the NullMemory baseline, and memory-induced violation rates show a robust upward trend with exposure length on both agent classes. Order-randomization experiments indicate that the effect is driven primarily by accumulated content rather than encounter order. Finally, a structural consequence of the event decomposition is that memory-induced risk is detectable from retrieval state before generation, which we confirm with a high-recall diagnostic monitor. Our results argue for treating memory safety as a longitudinal property that requires temporal evaluation, not a single-state property that can be captured by a snapshot.
2605.17830.paper_summary_zh: 安全評估配備記憶的 LLM 代理通常測量任務內的安全性：即代理是否能在單一情境下安全完成任務，通常是在如提示注入或記憶中毒等對抗條件下。然而，在部署中，單一代理在長期內服務於許多獨立任務，而在早期任務中累積的記憶可能會影響後續不相關任務的行為。研究這種情況需要沿著時間維度對任務進行評估：不是評估代理在任何單一記憶狀態下是否安全，而是隨著記憶在許多獨立互動中累積，其安全性質如何變化。我們稱這種失敗模式為時間記憶污染。為了將記憶暴露與流非穩定性隔離，我們引入了一種觸發探測協議，該協議評估固定的探測集對於不同前綴長度的只讀記憶快照，並結合 NullMemory 反事實基準來識別記憶引起的違規。我們在三個部署場景中應用這一協議，涵蓋記錄、備忘錄、表單和電子郵件通信，以及八種記憶架構，並額外在類似 Claw 的 AI 代理上進行應用，例如 OpenClaw，使用該平台的原生記憶機制。啟用記憶的代理始終超過 NullMemory 基準，且記憶引起的違規率在兩類代理中隨著暴露長度顯示出穩健的上升趨勢。隨機排序實驗表明，這一效應主要是由累積內容驅動，而非遭遇順序。最後，事件分解的結構性後果是，記憶引起的風險可以在生成之前從檢索狀態中檢測到，我們用高召回率的診斷監控器確認了這一點。我們的結果主張將記憶安全視為一種需要時間評估的縱向特性，而不是可以通過快照捕捉的單一狀態特性。
2605.17830.author: Ahmad Al-Tawaha et.al.
2605.17830.authors: Ahmad Al-Tawaha, Shangding Gu, Peizhi Niu, Ruoxi Jia, Ming Jin
